1	---
2	name: bead-lifecycle
3	description: Assess readiness, score, classify, and refine ddx beads. Used by ddx try/work hooks before claim or dispatch, after failed attempts, and for operator-invoked refinement.
4	---
5	
6	# Bead Lifecycle
7	
8	Assess readiness, score, classify, and refine DDx beads using the repository's
9	bead-authoring rubric. This skill is intentionally prompt-only: it gives agents
10	a stable contract for bead readiness checks before claim or dispatch,
11	failed-attempt triage after dispatch, and operator-invoked bead refinement.
12	
13	Invocation prompts MUST begin with one of:
14	
15	```text
16	MODE: readiness
17	MODE: intake
18	MODE: lint
19	MODE: triage
20	MODE: refine
21	```
22	
23	Read that first line and apply only the matching mode contract. Do not blend
24	mode outputs. Return only the requested structured output unless the caller asks
25	for explanatory prose.
26	
27	Ground all scoring in `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`, which
28	is canonical for the 8-criterion sufficient-sub-agent-prompt rubric.
29	
30	`MODE: intake` is the compatibility name for readiness mode. Treat it exactly
31	like `MODE: readiness` unless the caller gives a narrower schema.
32	
33	## READINESS MODE
34	
35	Use readiness mode before a bead is claimed or dispatched. The input is bead
36	JSON plus any available queue context, dependencies, prior attempt summaries,
37	or cheap repository evidence. The goal is to decide whether the bead is a
38	tractable, self-contained unit of work for an agent before DDx spends an
39	implementation attempt.
40	
41	Readiness mode answers a different question than infrastructure preflight.
42	Do not classify provider outages, quota exhaustion, missing harnesses,
43	transport failures, git index locks, worktree creation failures, ENOSPC, or
44	missing lifecycle automation as bead defects. Report those as system readiness
45	failures so DDx can pause, preflight, clean up, or fail open according to the
46	current policy.
47	
48	Check these bead-readiness failure reasons when the evidence is available:
49	
50	1. `too_large` — the bead bundles multiple independent implementation scopes,
51	   broad subsystem rewrites, or acceptance criteria that should be split into
52	   child beads.
53	2. `ambiguous_scope` — the requested behavior, ownership boundary, target file,
54	   or non-scope is unclear or contradictory.
55	3. `missing_root_cause_or_current_state` — a fix bead lacks file:line-grounded
56	   root cause, or a feature/docs bead lacks a concrete current-state anchor.
57	4. `missing_verification` — acceptance criteria lack named `Test*` symbols,
58	   unique `go test -run` filters, package-level `go test` commands, or
59	   `lefthook run pre-commit`, after legitimate waivers.
60	5. `missing_code_path_assertion` — introduced behavior has no wired assertion
61	   or reachable integration check, after legitimate waivers.
62	6. `missing_dependency_or_parent` — dependency, parent, spec-id, or external
63	   prerequisite information needed to execute safely is absent or inconsistent.
64	7. `hidden_external_blocker` — progress depends on credentials, service state,
65	   human decisions, generated artifacts, or upstream work that is not encoded
66	   as a dependency or blocker.
67	8. `already_satisfied_candidate` — cheap evidence strongly suggests the AC is
68	   already met and the attempt would be a no-op unless verification proves
69	   otherwise.
70	
71	Return JSON only:
72	
73	```json
74	{
75	  "classification": "ready|needs_refine|needs_split|needs_human|system_unready",
76	  "tractability": "tractable|too_large|ambiguous|blocked|unknown",
77	  "score": 0,
78	  "rationale": "brief evidence-grounded explanation",
79	  "readiness_checks": [
80	    {
81	      "reason": "too_large|ambiguous_scope|missing_root_cause_or_current_state|missing_verification|missing_code_path_assertion|missing_dependency_or_parent|hidden_external_blocker|already_satisfied_candidate|system_unready",
82	      "verdict": "pass|fail|unknown|waived",
83	      "evidence": "smallest durable evidence, preferably file:line or bead field",
84	      "checkable_before_attempt": true
85	    }
86	  ],
87	  "suggested_fixes": [
88	    {
89	      "target": "title|description|acceptance|labels|parent|deps|split|system",
90	      "fix": "specific amendment, split, or operator action"
91	    }
92	  ],
93	  "suggested_child_beads": [
94	    {
95	      "title": "imperative child title",
96	      "description": "standalone PROBLEM / ROOT CAUSE or CURRENT STATE / PROPOSED FIX / NON-SCOPE summary",
97	      "acceptance": [
98	        "1. Named verification criterion."
99	      ],
100	      "labels": [
101	        "phase:*",
102	        "area:*",
103	        "kind:*"
104	      ],
105	      "parent": "ddx-id",
106	      "deps": [
107	        "ddx-id: why"
108	      ]
109	    }
110	  ],
111	  "waivers_applied": [
112	    {
113	      "reason": "doc-only|epic|deletion|rename",
114	      "criteria": [
115	        "c"
116	      ],
117	      "evidence": "why the waiver is legitimate"
118	    }
119	  ]
120	}
121	```
122	
123	Use `ready` only when the bead is tractable and the sufficient-prompt rubric
124	passes after legitimate waivers. Use `needs_refine` when targeted metadata or
125	AC edits can make the bead ready without changing intent. Use `needs_split`
126	when child beads are required. Use `needs_human` when intent, scope, or
127	external state cannot be safely inferred. Use `system_unready` only when the
128	readiness assessment itself cannot run or the provided context proves an
129	infrastructure blocker rather than a bead defect.
130	
131	## LINT MODE
132	
133	Use lint mode before dispatch. The input is bead JSON: title, type, labels,
134	parent, deps, description, acceptance criteria, and any custom fields available.
135	
136	Rubric, scored one point each after applying waivers:
137	
138	1. Title is one-line scope clarity: imperative, names subsystem and change.
139	2. Description has PROBLEM, ROOT CAUSE or CURRENT STATE with file:line when
140	   applicable, PROPOSED FIX, and NON-SCOPE.
141	3. Acceptance criteria are numbered, verifiable, and name specific `Test*`
142	   symbols or a unique `go test -run` filter unless waived.
143	4. Acceptance criteria include a wired-in assertion for introduced code paths
144	   unless waived.
145	5. Acceptance criteria include both a `cd cli && go test ./<pkg>/...` command
146	   and `lefthook run pre-commit`.
147	6. Labels include phase, area, kind, and cross-reference facets.
148	7. Parent is explicit and dependencies are either listed or explicitly stated
149	   as "No deps."
150	8. The bead reads as a sufficient sub-agent prompt: a competent agent with only
151	   the bead body can pick files, edit scope, and verification commands without
152	   asking.
153	
154	Apply the rubric first, then apply any waiver from the waiver table only when
155	the bead type or labels clearly justify it. Do not use waivers to excuse vague
156	or missing context.
157	
158	Return JSON only:
159	
160	```json
161	{
162	  "score": 0,
163	  "rationale": [
164	    {
165	      "criterion": "a|b|c|d|e|f|g|h",
166	      "verdict": "pass|fail|waived",
167	      "reason": "brief evidence-grounded reason"
168	    }
169	  ],
170	  "suggested_fixes": [
171	    {
172	      "criterion": "a|b|c|d|e|f|g|h",
173	      "fix": "specific amendment to make"
174	    }
175	  ],
176	  "waivers_applied": [
177	    {
178	      "criterion": "c|d|implementation",
179	      "waiver": "doc-only|epic|deletion",
180	      "reason": "why this bead qualifies"
181	    }
182	  ]
183	}
184	```
185	
186	`score` is the number of pass or waived criteria after legitimate waivers. Use
187	integer scores from 0 through 8.
188	
189	## TRIAGE MODE
190	
191	Use triage mode after an attempt ends without straightforward success. The input
192	is the bead, an outcome event, and a relevant session log excerpt. Classify the
193	failure and recommend the next queue action.
194	
195	Triage mode is advisory. It classifies evidence and recommends a TD-031 action
196	category; final queue mutation semantics are owned by
197	`docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md` and the
198	`ddx try` / `ddx work` implementation. Do not invent persisted statuses or
199	queue mutation rules in skill output.
200	
201	Valid classifications:
202	
203	- `already_satisfied` — repository already meets the bead AC.
204	- `no_changes_unverified` — no-change evidence included verification, but it
205	  failed or could not run.
206	- `no_changes_unjustified` — no-change evidence lacks enough structured
207	  rationale to prove satisfaction or a durable blocker.
208	- `needs_investigation` — evidence is ambiguous or asks for operator triage.
209	- `decomposed` — parent/epic/container work was decomposed or should be made
210	  execution-ineligible for ordinary queue execution.
211	- `blocked` — a hard external precondition prevents progress.
212	- `superseded` — work has been replaced by another bead or artifact.
213	- `routing` — model/provider/harness selection or capability mismatch.
214	- `quota` — rate limit, spend cap, or usage ceiling.
215	- `transport` — network, API, subprocess, serialization, or connector failure.
216	- `tests_red` — implementation exists but verification failed.
217	- `merge_conflict` — landing failed due to git conflicts.
218	- `review_block` — reviewer found blocking issues or requested changes.
219	- `timeout` — attempt exceeded time or idle limits.
220	- `recoverable` — transient infrastructure or time-based condition can plausibly
221	  succeed by retrying the same bead later.
222	
223	Valid recommended actions:
224	
225	- `close_already_satisfied`
226	- `release_claim_retry`
227	- `release_claim_needs_investigation`
228	- `release_claim_mark_blocked`
229	- `release_claim_mark_superseded`
230	- `release_claim_wait_retry`
231	- `close_decomposed_or_mark_execution_ineligible`
232	
233	Prefer the narrowest classification supported by the evidence. If the log shows
234	both a vague bead and a tool timeout, classify the first event that explains why
235	work could not be completed reliably.
236	
237	Do not collapse recurring system failures into bead-quality failures. Recent
238	attempt evidence should be classified as follows:
239	
240	- Provider exhaustion, quota ceilings, missing viable provider, missing harness,
241	  or transport errors are `routing`, `quota`, or `transport`.
242	- ENOSPC, failed worktree creation, evidence write failures, and git index lock
243	  contention are `recoverable` infrastructure failures unless the log proves a
244	  deterministic repository defect.
245	- A pre-execute checkpoint or pre-commit failure before implementation starts
246	  is `recoverable` or `needs_investigation` unless the failing test output
247	  identifies a bead-owned code regression.
248	- `no_changes` with passing verification is `already_satisfied`; `no_changes`
249	  without enough proof is `no_changes_unjustified`.
250	- Red tests after the worker changed code are `tests_red`.
251	
252	Return JSON only:
253	
254	```json
255	{
256	  "classification": "already_satisfied|no_changes_unverified|no_changes_unjustified|needs_investigation|decomposed|blocked|superseded|routing|quota|transport|tests_red|merge_conflict|review_block|timeout|recoverable",
257	  "recommended_action": "close_already_satisfied|release_claim_retry|release_claim_needs_investigation|release_claim_mark_blocked|release_claim_mark_superseded|release_claim_wait_retry|close_decomposed_or_mark_execution_ineligible",
258	  "rationale": "brief evidence-grounded explanation",
259	  "suggested_amendments": [
260	    {
261	      "target": "title|description|acceptance|labels|parent|deps",
262	      "amendment": "specific proposed change"
263	    }
264	  ],
265	  "suggested_followup_beads": [
266	    {
267	      "title": "imperative child or follow-up title",
268	      "description": "standalone problem/root-cause/proposed-fix/non-scope summary",
269	      "acceptance": [
270	        "numbered AC line with named verification"
271	      ],
272	      "labels": [
273	        "phase:N",
274	        "area:*",
275	        "kind:*"
276	      ],
277	      "parent": "ddx-id or empty when unknown",
278	      "deps": [
279	        "ddx-id: why"
280	      ]
281	    }
282	  ]
283	}
284	```
285	
286	Use an empty `suggested_followup_beads` array when no child or follow-up bead is
287	needed. Suggested follow-up beads must be execution-ready drafts, not vague
288	reminders.
289	
290	## REFINE MODE
291	
292	Use refine mode when an operator asks to amend a bead before retry. The input is
293	the bead and optionally a prior triage output. Produce a YAML diff describing
294	only recommended tracker amendments. Do not run `ddx bead update`; this mode is
295	advisory unless the caller separately asks you to mutate the tracker.
296	
297	Return YAML only:
298	
299	```yaml
300	title:
301	  from: "current title"
302	  to: "refined imperative title"
303	description:
304	  add:
305	    - section: "PROBLEM"
306	      text: "standalone text to add"
307	    - section: "ROOT CAUSE"
308	      text: "file:line-grounded root cause or CURRENT STATE for features"
309	  replace:
310	    - from: "ambiguous existing sentence"
311	      to: "specific replacement"
312	acceptance:
313	  add:
314	    - "N. TestSpecificName verifies the behavior."
315	    - "N+1. cd cli && go test ./internal/pkg/... passes."
316	    - "N+2. lefthook run pre-commit passes."
317	  remove:
318	    - "vague or duplicate AC line"
319	labels:
320	  add:
321	    - "area:subsystem"
322	    - "kind:fix"
323	  remove:
324	    - "misleading-label"
325	parent:
326	  from: "old-parent-or-empty"
327	  to: "new-parent-or-empty"
328	deps:
329	  add:
330	    - "ddx-id: why this dependency matters"
331	  remove:
332	    - "ddx-id: why it is not a true dependency"
333	notes:
334	  - "short explanation of any waiver or non-obvious judgment"
335	```
336	
337	Equivalent JSON output contract for callers that request JSON instead of YAML:
338	
339	```json
340	{
341	  "title": {
342	    "from": "current title",
343	    "to": "refined imperative title"
344	  },
345	  "description": {
346	    "add": [
347	      {
348	        "section": "PROBLEM|ROOT CAUSE|CURRENT STATE|PROPOSED FIX|NON-SCOPE",
349	        "text": "standalone text to add"
350	      }
351	    ],
352	    "replace": [
353	      {
354	        "from": "ambiguous existing sentence",
355	        "to": "specific replacement"
356	      }
357	    ]
358	  },
359	  "acceptance": {
360	    "add": [
361	      "N. TestSpecificName verifies the behavior.",
362	      "N+1. cd cli && go test ./internal/pkg/... passes.",
363	      "N+2. lefthook run pre-commit passes."
364	    ],
365	    "remove": [
366	      "vague or duplicate AC line"
367	    ]
368	  },
369	  "labels": {
370	    "add": [
371	      "area:subsystem",
372	      "kind:fix"
373	    ],
374	    "remove": [
375	      "misleading-label"
376	    ]
377	  },
378	  "parent": {
379	    "from": "old-parent-or-empty",
380	    "to": "new-parent-or-empty"
381	  },
382	  "deps": {
383	    "add": [
384	      "ddx-id: why this dependency matters"
385	    ],
386	    "remove": [
387	      "ddx-id: why it is not a true dependency"
388	    ]
389	  },
390	  "notes": [
391	    "short explanation of any waiver or non-obvious judgment"
392	  ]
393	}
394	```
395	
396	Omit YAML keys that have no proposed changes. Keep replacements specific enough
397	that an operator can translate them directly into `ddx bead update` and
398	`ddx bead dep` commands.
399	
400	## WAIVER TABLE
401	
402	Rubric-first, label override second: score the bead against all eight criteria,
403	then apply these waivers only when the bead type, labels, and content make the
404	waiver defensible.
405	
406	| Bead type or label | Criterion skip | Conditions |
407	|---|---|---|
408	| `kind:doc`, `kind:docs`, or doc-only scope | Criterion (c), criterion (d) | The bead changes documentation only, names the doc path, includes `lefthook run pre-commit`, and remains sufficient for a documentation agent. |
409	| `type: epic` or `kind:epic` | Specific test-name part of criterion (c), criterion (d), concrete-implementation expectation | The bead is an aggregate container, lists child scope or decomposition criteria, includes parent/deps status, and names the collective verification gate expected from children. |
410	| `kind:deletion`, `kind:rename`, `kind:cleanup`, or deletion/rename scope | Criterion (d) | The bead cites the target file:line, states behavior preservation or removal intent, and acceptance criteria verify no stale references remain. |
411	
412	Never waive criterion (h). A bead must still be a sufficient prompt for its
413	actual type.
414	
415	## Examples
416	
417	Curated examples live in `examples/`. Use them as calibration cases for lint,
418	triage, and refine output shape:
419	
420	- `code-bug-lint.json`
421	- `feature-lint.json`
422	- `doc-only-waiver.json`
423	- `epic-waiver.json`
424	- `deletion-rename-waiver.json`
425	- `no-op-investigation-triage.json`
426	- `upstream-external-triage.json`

1	---
2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
11	orchestrates work while Fizeau routes providers/models, catalog lookups,
12	and transcript rendering), a persona system (bindable AI
13	personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
14	personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
15	skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
16	CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.
17	
18	## How this skill works
19	
20	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
21	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
22	
23	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
24	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
25	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
26	guidance, not this overview alone.**
27	
28	## Vocabulary
29	
30	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
31	exact definitions.
32	
33	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
34	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
35	- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
36	  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
37	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
38	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
39	- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
40	  `ddx bead blocked`.
41	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
42	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
43	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
44	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
45	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
46	- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
47	  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
48	  passthrough constraints.
49	- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
50	  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
51	  finalization.
52	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
53	  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
54	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
55	  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
56	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
57	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
58	  below).
59	- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
60	  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
61	  branch on harness names.
62	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
63	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
64	  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
65	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
66	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
67	  personas.
68	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
69	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
70	- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
71	  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
72	  Fizeau owns the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
73	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
74	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
75	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
76	  `ddx install <name>`.
77	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
78	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
79	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
80	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
81	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
82	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
83	  orchestration; that remains harness business.
84	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
85	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
86	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
87	  `ddx upgrade`.
88	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
89	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
90	  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
91	  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
92	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
93	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
94	  via `spec-id`.
95	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
96	  at its governing artifact.
97	
98	## Intent router
99	
100	Before responding, read the matching file.
101	
102	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
103	|---|---|
104	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
105	| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
106	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
107	| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
108	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
109	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
110	
111	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
112	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
113	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
114	
115	## Top-level policy reminders
116	
117	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
118	reference file; do not violate them.
119	
120	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
121	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
122	  bead history and cannot be audited.
123	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
124	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
125	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
126	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
127	- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
128	  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
129	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
130	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
131	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
132	  branches.

134	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
135	  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
136	  manual parallel work should too.

138	  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
139	  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.

9	# ADR-023: Bead Lifecycle Quality Policy
10	
11	**Status:** Proposed (Accepted after operator review)
12	**Date:** 2026-05-04
13	**Authors:** bead `ddx-9210f95a`
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	Reliability principle P7 ("BEAD = PROMPT") is planned in
18	`docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md` by bead `ddx-06b77652`.
19	The principle says a bead's description and acceptance criteria must be enough
20	for a competent sub-agent to execute without hand-curation, but it does not
21	define the enforcement mechanism, waiver model, staged rollout, or recovery UX.
22	
23	The quality gap is already visible in evidence:
24	
25	- Audit 1, `.ddx/executions/20260503T195715-4725673a/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`,
26	  found repeated misses in root-cause detail, concrete test names, and
27	  self-contained scope.
28	- Audit 1 snapshot,
29	  `.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`,
30	  directly operationalizes P7 and records the first 20-bead scoring pass.
31	- Audit 2,
32	  `.ddx/executions/20260504T030000-audit2/bead-quality-audit-remaining-2026-05-04.md`,
33	  extended the scan across the remaining open queue and calls out evidence bead
34	  `ddx-f339c399`.
35	- `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` is the canonical
36	  8-criterion authoring rubric and explicitly defines valid criterion skips for
37	  epic, doc-only, deletion, and rename beads.
38	
39	Three existing feature specs already own the affected surfaces:
40	
41	- FEAT-004 owns bead schema, validation hooks, labels, and evidence.
42	- FEAT-010 owns `ddx try`, `ddx work`, run records, and attempt outcomes.
43	- FEAT-011 owns the DDx skill packaging model that gives agents reusable
44	  workflow guidance.
45	
46	## Decision
47	
48	DDx adopts bead-lifecycle quality enforcement as policy over the existing
49	bead, execution, and skill surfaces. No new bead schema fields are introduced.
50	No new top-level FEAT is created.
51	
52	### Policy
53	
54	Every automated bead attempt has two quality checkpoints:
55	
56	1. **Bead readiness assessment.** Before `ddx work` claims a bead or `ddx try`
57	   launches the implementation invocation, DDx evaluates whether the bead is
58	   tractable and actionable. It checks the bead description, acceptance
59	   criteria, labels, parent, dependency metadata, spec-id, prior attempt
60	   history, and the canonical rubric in
61	   `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`. The implementation hook
62	   may still advertise `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility only; the
63	   canonical product concept is bead readiness assessment.
64	2. **Post-attempt triage.** After an attempt finalizes, DDx triages the result
65	   against the same lifecycle quality policy so a low-quality prompt failure,
66	   missing rationale, empty review block, or structurally ambiguous outcome is
67	   classified and surfaced consistently.
68	
69	The readiness checkpoint and the post-attempt triage checkpoint are distinct:
70	readiness decides whether a bead should be claimed or rewritten before
71	execution, lint/rubric scoring measures prompt quality inside the readiness
72	evaluation, and triage classifies the attempt after evidence exists.
73	
74	Both checkpoints invoke the same nested bead-lifecycle workflow skill under the
75	`ddx` skill tree. The skill is responsible for translating the rubric into
76	agent-readable findings; DDx remains responsible for hook timing, evidence
77	placement, and outcome classification.
78	
79	Post-attempt triage classifications feed
80	[`TD-031`](../technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md). ADR-023 does not
81	define final queue mutation policy; TD-031 remains the source of truth for
82	whether an attempt closes, stays open for human triage, becomes blocked, is
83	superseded, or receives a retry cooldown.
84	
85	### Staged Rollout And Factory Mode
86	
87	The default rollout is WARN-ONLY.
88	
89	- WARN-ONLY mode reports the lint score, missing criteria, waiver matches, and
90	  suggested remediation, then proceeds with dispatch.
91	- BLOCK mode is opt-in until the queue baseline confirms that ordinary
92	  execution-ready beads can consistently satisfy the rubric.
93	- BLOCK mode may stop only on a valid low lint score after applicable rubric
94	  skips and label waivers are applied. It must not stop on hook crashes,
95	  missing skill files, transient filesystem errors, or malformed lint output.
96	- Reliable factory mode is BLOCK mode plus default adversarial review. In that
97	  mode, poorly specified work is improved, decomposed, or blocked before claim
98	  rather than allowed to consume implementation attempts.
99	
100	This makes the policy measurable before it becomes a dispatch gate and avoids
101	freezing the queue while legacy beads are being retrofitted.
102	
103	### Waiver Model
104	
105	Waivers exist at two levels:
106	
107	- **Rubric-level by bead type.** The skips documented in
108	  `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` are built into lint:
109	  doc-only beads may omit criterion (c) and (d), epic beads may satisfy those
110	  criteria through children, and pure deletion or rename beads may omit
111	  criterion (d) while preserving behavior.
112	- **Per-bead labels.** Rare exceptions use labels of the form
113	  `lint-waiver:<criterion>`, for example `lint-waiver:c`. Labels are the
114	  durable waiver store because FEAT-004 already defines labels as free-form
115	  bead metadata and no schema change is warranted.
116	
117	Operator override uses `--force --reason <text>`. The override records an event
118	in the bead evidence stream naming the criterion, mode, actor, reason, and lint
119	summary. It does not silently bypass the hook and it does not mutate the rubric.
120	
121	### Fail-Open Behavior
122	
123	The lifecycle hooks follow reliability principle P1's fail-open posture for
124	infrastructure failures.
125	
126	- If the hook cannot run, the workflow skill is missing, output cannot be
127	  parsed, or evidence cannot be written, DDx records the infrastructure failure
128	  and proceeds.
129	- In WARN-ONLY mode, all valid lint results proceed after reporting.
130	- In BLOCK mode, only a valid post-waiver lint result below the configured
131	  threshold blocks dispatch.
132	- Post-attempt triage failures never erase the attempt result. They annotate the
133	  report and evidence so retry policy and operators can distinguish "agent made
134	  a bad attempt" from "quality infrastructure failed."
135	
136	### Safe Improvement, Decomposition, And Recovery UX
137	
138	Bead readiness assessment may update a bead before execution only when the
139	update is intent-preserving and grounded in durable context. Safe improvements
140	include normalizing the description into the authoring template, adding
141	discovered file:line evidence, adding an obvious subsystem test command, and
142	wiring deterministic labels, parent, or dependencies. Readiness must block for
143	human input instead of inventing acceptance criteria, changing scope, choosing
144	between conflicting requirements, or guessing a missing governing artifact.
145	
146	If readiness finds the bead too broad, it decomposes before claim. Every parent AC
147	must map to at least one child AC or be explicitly marked `needs_human` or
148	`non_scope`; token-overlap metrics are heuristics, not proof of preservation.
149	The parent is blocked/decomposed with child ids and the AC map in evidence.
150	
151	When BLOCK mode stops dispatch, the operator-facing output must be actionable.
152	It prints:
153	
154	- bead id, score, mode, and blocked criteria
155	- the missing fields or malformed sections in plain language
156	- the waiver labels already applied and the labels that would waive each
157	  failed criterion
158	- suggested `ddx bead update` commands for adding description, acceptance,
159	  labels, parent, notes, or dependency metadata
160	- the `--force --reason` form for exceptional dispatch, with the note that an
161	  evidence event will be recorded
162	
163	Recovery output must not require reading the lint implementation or an
164	out-of-band report to fix an ordinary authoring problem.
165	
166	## Consequences
167	
168	- FEAT-004 owns the label-based waiver storage and the no-new-schema rule.
169	- FEAT-010 owns hook insertion in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, attempt
170	  classification, and the `OutcomeReason` report field.
171	- FEAT-011 owns nested workflow-skill packaging so the same rubric guidance can
172	  be reused by lint, triage, review, breakdown, replay, and benchmark flows.
173	- Legacy beads can continue running in WARN-ONLY while the queue is baselined.
174	- Once BLOCK mode is enabled, dispatch quality becomes explicit and auditable
175	  instead of depending on operator memory.
176	
177	## Non-Goals
178	
179	- A new FEAT for bead quality; the existing feature specs own the affected
180	  surfaces.
181	- The separate `in_progress` eligibility bug.
182	- Cross-project skill packaging, which remains deferred to FEAT-015.
183	- New fields in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`; waivers use labels and evidence events.
184	
185	## References
186	
187	- `ddx-06b77652` — RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES bead for P7.
188	- `ddx-f339c399` — evidence bead called out by Audit 2.
189	- `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` — canonical rubric and
190	  criterion-skip policy.
191	- `.ddx/executions/20260503T195715-4725673a/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`
192	  — Audit 1.
193	- `.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`
194	  — Audit 1 scoring snapshot.
195	- `.ddx/executions/20260504T030000-audit2/bead-quality-audit-remaining-2026-05-04.md`
196	  — Audit 2.
197	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-beads.md` — bead metadata,
198	  validation, labels, and evidence.
199	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-task-execution.md` — try/work lifecycle
200	  and attempt outcomes.
201	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-011-skills.md` — DDx skill packaging.

8	Assess readiness, score, classify, and refine DDx beads using the repository's
9	bead-authoring rubric. This skill is intentionally prompt-only: it gives agents
10	a stable contract for bead readiness checks before claim or dispatch,
11	failed-attempt triage after dispatch, and operator-invoked bead refinement.

13	Invocation prompts MUST begin with one of:
14

16	MODE: readiness
17	MODE: intake
18	MODE: lint
19	MODE: triage
20	MODE: refine
21	```
22	
23	Read that first line and apply only the matching mode contract. Do not blend
24	mode outputs. Return only the requested structured output unless the caller asks
25	for explanatory prose.
26	
27	Ground all scoring in `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`, which
28	is canonical for the 8-criterion sufficient-sub-agent-prompt rubric.
29	
30	`MODE: intake` is the compatibility name for readiness mode. Treat it exactly
31	like `MODE: readiness` unless the caller gives a narrower schema.
32	
33	## READINESS MODE
34	
35	Use readiness mode before a bead is claimed or dispatched. The input is bead
36	JSON plus any available queue context, dependencies, prior attempt summaries,
37	or cheap repository evidence. The goal is to decide whether the bead is a
38	tractable, self-contained unit of work for an agent before DDx spends an
39	implementation attempt.
40	
41	Readiness mode answers a different question than infrastructure preflight.
42	Do not classify provider outages, quota exhaustion, missing harnesses,
43	transport failures, git index locks, worktree creation failures, ENOSPC, or
44	missing lifecycle automation as bead defects. Report those as system readiness
45	failures so DDx can pause, preflight, clean up, or fail open according to the
46	current policy.
47	
48	Check these bead-readiness failure reasons when the evidence is available:
49	
50	1. `too_large` — the bead bundles multiple independent implementation scopes,
51	   broad subsystem rewrites, or acceptance criteria that should be split into
52	   child beads.
53	2. `ambiguous_scope` — the requested behavior, ownership boundary, target file,
54	   or non-scope is unclear or contradictory.
55	3. `missing_root_cause_or_current_state` — a fix bead lacks file:line-grounded
56	   root cause, or a feature/docs bead lacks a concrete current-state anchor.
57	4. `missing_verification` — acceptance criteria lack named `Test*` symbols,
58	   unique `go test -run` filters, package-level `go test` commands, or
59	   `lefthook run pre-commit`, after legitimate waivers.
60	5. `missing_code_path_assertion` — introduced behavior has no wired assertion
61	   or reachable integration check, after legitimate waivers.
62	6. `missing_dependency_or_parent` — dependency, parent, spec-id, or external
63	   prerequisite information needed to execute safely is absent or inconsistent.
64	7. `hidden_external_blocker` — progress depends on credentials, service state,
65	   human decisions, generated artifacts, or upstream work that is not encoded
66	   as a dependency or blocker.
67	8. `already_satisfied_candidate` — cheap evidence strongly suggests the AC is

131	## LINT MODE
132	
133	Use lint mode before dispatch. The input is bead JSON: title, type, labels,
134	parent, deps, description, acceptance criteria, and any custom fields available.
135	
136	Rubric, scored one point each after applying waivers:
137	
138	1. Title is one-line scope clarity: imperative, names subsystem and change.
139	2. Description has PROBLEM, ROOT CAUSE or CURRENT STATE with file:line when
140	   applicable, PROPOSED FIX, and NON-SCOPE.
141	3. Acceptance criteria are numbered, verifiable, and name specific `Test*`
142	   symbols or a unique `go test -run` filter unless waived.
143	4. Acceptance criteria include a wired-in assertion for introduced code paths
144	   unless waived.
145	5. Acceptance criteria include both a `cd cli && go test ./<pkg>/...` command
146	   and `lefthook run pre-commit`.
147	6. Labels include phase, area, kind, and cross-reference facets.
148	7. Parent is explicit and dependencies are either listed or explicitly stated
149	   as "No deps."
150	8. The bead reads as a sufficient sub-agent prompt: a competent agent with only
151	   the bead body can pick files, edit scope, and verification commands without
152	   asking.
153	
154	Apply the rubric first, then apply any waiver from the waiver table only when
155	the bead type or labels clearly justify it. Do not use waivers to excuse vague
156	or missing context.

163	  "rationale": [

166	      "verdict": "pass|fail|waived",
167	      "reason": "brief evidence-grounded reason"
168	    }
169	  ],
170	  "suggested_fixes": [

172	      "criterion": "a|b|c|d|e|f|g|h",
173	      "fix": "specific amendment to make"
174	    }
175	  ],

181	    }
182	  ]
183	}
184	```
185	
186	`score` is the number of pass or waived criteria after legitimate waivers. Use
187	integer scores from 0 through 8.
188	
189	## TRIAGE MODE
190	
191	Use triage mode after an attempt ends without straightforward success. The input
192	is the bead, an outcome event, and a relevant session log excerpt. Classify the
193	failure and recommend the next queue action.
194	
195	Triage mode is advisory. It classifies evidence and recommends a TD-031 action
196	category; final queue mutation semantics are owned by
197	`docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md` and the
198	`ddx try` / `ddx work` implementation. Do not invent persisted statuses or
199	queue mutation rules in skill output.
200	
201	Valid classifications:
202	
203	- `already_satisfied` — repository already meets the bead AC.
204	- `no_changes_unverified` — no-change evidence included verification, but it
205	  failed or could not run.
206	- `no_changes_unjustified` — no-change evidence lacks enough structured
207	  rationale to prove satisfaction or a durable blocker.
208	- `needs_investigation` — evidence is ambiguous or asks for operator triage.
209	- `decomposed` — parent/epic/container work was decomposed or should be made
210	  execution-ineligible for ordinary queue execution.
211	- `blocked` — a hard external precondition prevents progress.
212	- `superseded` — work has been replaced by another bead or artifact.
213	- `routing` — model/provider/harness selection or capability mismatch.
214	- `quota` — rate limit, spend cap, or usage ceiling.
215	- `transport` — network, API, subprocess, serialization, or connector failure.
216	- `tests_red` — implementation exists but verification failed.
217	- `merge_conflict` — landing failed due to git conflicts.
218	- `review_block` — reviewer found blocking issues or requested changes.
219	- `timeout` — attempt exceeded time or idle limits.
220	- `recoverable` — transient infrastructure or time-based condition can plausibly
221	  succeed by retrying the same bead later.

225	- `close_already_satisfied`
226	- `release_claim_retry`
227	- `release_claim_needs_investigation`

229	- `release_claim_mark_superseded`
230	- `release_claim_wait_retry`
231	- `close_decomposed_or_mark_execution_ineligible`
232	
233	Prefer the narrowest classification supported by the evidence. If the log shows
234	both a vague bead and a tool timeout, classify the first event that explains why
235	work could not be completed reliably.
236	
237	Do not collapse recurring system failures into bead-quality failures. Recent
238	attempt evidence should be classified as follows:
239	
240	- Provider exhaustion, quota ceilings, missing viable provider, missing harness,
241	  or transport errors are `routing`, `quota`, or `transport`.
242	- ENOSPC, failed worktree creation, evidence write failures, and git index lock
243	  contention are `recoverable` infrastructure failures unless the log proves a
244	  deterministic repository defect.
245	- A pre-execute checkpoint or pre-commit failure before implementation starts
246	  is `recoverable` or `needs_investigation` unless the failing test output
247	  identifies a bead-owned code regression.
248	- `no_changes` with passing verification is `already_satisfied`; `no_changes`
249	  without enough proof is `no_changes_unjustified`.
250	- Red tests after the worker changed code are `tests_red`.
251	
252	Return JSON only:
253	
254	```json
255	{
256	  "classification": "already_satisfied|no_changes_unverified|no_changes_unjustified|needs_investigation|decomposed|blocked|superseded|routing|quota|transport|tests_red|merge_conflict|review_block|timeout|recoverable",
257	  "recommended_action": "close_already_satisfied|release_claim_retry|release_claim_needs_investigation|release_claim_mark_blocked|release_claim_mark_superseded|release_claim_wait_retry|close_decomposed_or_mark_execution_ineligible",
258	  "rationale": "brief evidence-grounded explanation",
259	  "suggested_amendments": [
260	    {
261	      "target": "title|description|acceptance|labels|parent|deps",
262	      "amendment": "specific proposed change"
263	    }
264	  ],
265	  "suggested_followup_beads": [
266	    {
267	      "title": "imperative child or follow-up title",
268	      "description": "standalone problem/root-cause/proposed-fix/non-scope summary",
269	      "acceptance": [
270	        "numbered AC line with named verification"
271	      ],
272	      "labels": [
273	        "phase:N",
274	        "area:*",
275	        "kind:*"
276	      ],
277	      "parent": "ddx-id or empty when unknown",
278	      "deps": [
279	        "ddx-id: why"
280	      ]
281	    }
282	  ]
283	}
284	```
285	
286	Use an empty `suggested_followup_beads` array when no child or follow-up bead is
287	needed. Suggested follow-up beads must be execution-ready drafts, not vague
288	reminders.
289	
290	## REFINE MODE
291	
292	Use refine mode when an operator asks to amend a bead before retry. The input is
293	the bead and optionally a prior triage output. Produce a YAML diff describing
294	only recommended tracker amendments. Do not run `ddx bead update`; this mode is
295	advisory unless the caller separately asks you to mutate the tracker.
296	
297	Return YAML only:
298	
299	```yaml
300	title:
301	  from: "current title"
302	  to: "refined imperative title"
303	description:
304	  add:
305	    - section: "PROBLEM"
306	      text: "standalone text to add"
307	    - section: "ROOT CAUSE"
308	      text: "file:line-grounded root cause or CURRENT STATE for features"
309	  replace:
310	    - from: "ambiguous existing sentence"
311	      to: "specific replacement"
312	acceptance:
313	  add:
314	    - "N. TestSpecificName verifies the behavior."
315	    - "N+1. cd cli && go test ./internal/pkg/... passes."
316	    - "N+2. lefthook run pre-commit passes."
317	  remove:
318	    - "vague or duplicate AC line"
319	labels:
320	  add:
321	    - "area:subsystem"
322	    - "kind:fix"
323	  remove:
324	    - "misleading-label"
325	parent:
326	  from: "old-parent-or-empty"
327	  to: "new-parent-or-empty"
328	deps:
329	  add:
330	    - "ddx-id: why this dependency matters"
331	  remove:
332	    - "ddx-id: why it is not a true dependency"
333	notes:
334	  - "short explanation of any waiver or non-obvious judgment"
335	```
336	
337	Equivalent JSON output contract for callers that request JSON instead of YAML:
338	
339	```json
340	{
341	  "title": {
342	    "from": "current title",
343	    "to": "refined imperative title"
344	  },
345	  "description": {
346	    "add": [
347	      {
348	        "section": "PROBLEM|ROOT CAUSE|CURRENT STATE|PROPOSED FIX|NON-SCOPE",
349	        "text": "standalone text to add"
350	      }
351	    ],
352	    "replace": [
353	      {
354	        "from": "ambiguous existing sentence",
355	        "to": "specific replacement"
356	      }
357	    ]
358	  },
359	  "acceptance": {
360	    "add": [
361	      "N. TestSpecificName verifies the behavior.",
362	      "N+1. cd cli && go test ./internal/pkg/... passes.",
363	      "N+2. lefthook run pre-commit passes."
364	    ],
365	    "remove": [
366	      "vague or duplicate AC line"
367	    ]
368	  },
369	  "labels": {
370	    "add": [
371	      "area:subsystem",
372	      "kind:fix"
373	    ],
374	    "remove": [
375	      "misleading-label"
376	    ]
377	  },
378	  "parent": {
379	    "from": "old-parent-or-empty",
380	    "to": "new-parent-or-empty"
381	  },
382	  "deps": {
383	    "add": [
384	      "ddx-id: why this dependency matters"
385	    ],
386	    "remove": [
387	      "ddx-id: why it is not a true dependency"
388	    ]
389	  },
390	  "notes": [
391	    "short explanation of any waiver or non-obvious judgment"
392	  ]
393	}
394	```
395	
396	Omit YAML keys that have no proposed changes. Keep replacements specific enough
397	that an operator can translate them directly into `ddx bead update` and
398	`ddx bead dep` commands.
399	
400	## WAIVER TABLE
401	
402	Rubric-first, label override second: score the bead against all eight criteria,
403	then apply these waivers only when the bead type, labels, and content make the
404	waiver defensible.
405	
406	| Bead type or label | Criterion skip | Conditions |
407	|---|---|---|
408	| `kind:doc`, `kind:docs`, or doc-only scope | Criterion (c), criterion (d) | The bead changes documentation only, names the doc path, includes `lefthook run pre-commit`, and remains sufficient for a documentation agent. |
409	| `type: epic` or `kind:epic` | Specific test-name part of criterion (c), criterion (d), concrete-implementation expectation | The bead is an aggregate container, lists child scope or decomposition criteria, includes parent/deps status, and names the collective verification gate expected from children. |
410	| `kind:deletion`, `kind:rename`, `kind:cleanup`, or deletion/rename scope | Criterion (d) | The bead cites the target file:line, states behavior preservation or removal intent, and acceptance criteria verify no stale references remain. |
411	
412	Never waive criterion (h). A bead must still be a sufficient prompt for its
413	actual type.
414	
415	## Examples
416	
417	Curated examples live in `examples/`. Use them as calibration cases for lint,
418	triage, and refine output shape:
419	
420	- `code-bug-lint.json`
421	- `feature-lint.json`
422	- `doc-only-waiver.json`
423	- `epic-waiver.json`
424	- `deletion-rename-waiver.json`
425	- `no-op-investigation-triage.json`
426	- `upstream-external-triage.json`

2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
11	orchestrates work while Fizeau routes providers/models, catalog lookups,
12	and transcript rendering), a persona system (bindable AI
13	personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
14	personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
15	skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
16	CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.
17	
18	## How this skill works
19	
20	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
21	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
22	
23	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
24	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
25	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
26	guidance, not this overview alone.**
27	
28	## Vocabulary
29	
30	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
31	exact definitions.
32	
33	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
34	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
35	- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
36	  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
37	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
38	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
39	- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
40	  `ddx bead blocked`.
41	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
42	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
43	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
44	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
45	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
46	- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
47	  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
48	  passthrough constraints.
49	- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
50	  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
51	  finalization.
52	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
53	  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
54	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
55	  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
56	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
57	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
58	  below).
59	- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
60	  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
61	  branch on harness names.
62	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
63	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
64	  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
65	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
66	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
67	  personas.
68	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
69	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
70	- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
71	  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
72	  Fizeau owns the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
73	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
74	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
75	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
76	  `ddx install <name>`.
77	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
78	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
79	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
80	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
81	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
82	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
83	  orchestration; that remains harness business.
84	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
85	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
86	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
87	  `ddx upgrade`.
88	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
89	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
90	  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
91	  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
92	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
93	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
94	  via `spec-id`.
95	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
96	  at its governing artifact.
97	
98	## Intent router
99	
100	Before responding, read the matching file.
101	
102	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
103	|---|---|
104	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
105	| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
106	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
107	| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
108	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
109	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
110	
111	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
112	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
113	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
114	
115	## Top-level policy reminders
116	
117	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
118	reference file; do not violate them.
119	
120	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
121	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
122	  bead history and cannot be audited.
123	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
124	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
125	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
126	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
127	- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
128	  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
129	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
130	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
131	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
132	  branches.
133	- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
134	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
135	  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
136	  manual parallel work should too.
137	- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
138	  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
139	  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.
140	
141	## Links out
142	
143	- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
144	- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
145	  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
146	  executions, and skills features.
147	- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
148	  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
149	- Open standard this skill conforms to:
150	  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).
151

8	---
9	# ADR-023: Bead Lifecycle Quality Policy
10	
11	**Status:** Proposed (Accepted after operator review)
12	**Date:** 2026-05-04
13	**Authors:** bead `ddx-9210f95a`
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	Reliability principle P7 ("BEAD = PROMPT") is planned in
18	`docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md` by bead `ddx-06b77652`.
19	The principle says a bead's description and acceptance criteria must be enough
20	for a competent sub-agent to execute without hand-curation, but it does not
21	define the enforcement mechanism, waiver model, staged rollout, or recovery UX.
22	
23	The quality gap is already visible in evidence:
24	
25	- Audit 1, `.ddx/executions/20260503T195715-4725673a/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`,
26	  found repeated misses in root-cause detail, concrete test names, and
27	  self-contained scope.
28	- Audit 1 snapshot,
29	  `.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`,
30	  directly operationalizes P7 and records the first 20-bead scoring pass.
31	- Audit 2,
32	  `.ddx/executions/20260504T030000-audit2/bead-quality-audit-remaining-2026-05-04.md`,
33	  extended the scan across the remaining open queue and calls out evidence bead
34	  `ddx-f339c399`.
35	- `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` is the canonical
36	  8-criterion authoring rubric and explicitly defines valid criterion skips for
37	  epic, doc-only, deletion, and rename beads.
38	
39	Three existing feature specs already own the affected surfaces:
40	
41	- FEAT-004 owns bead schema, validation hooks, labels, and evidence.
42	- FEAT-010 owns `ddx try`, `ddx work`, run records, and attempt outcomes.
43	- FEAT-011 owns the DDx skill packaging model that gives agents reusable
44	  workflow guidance.
45	
46	## Decision
47	
48	DDx adopts bead-lifecycle quality enforcement as policy over the existing
49	bead, execution, and skill surfaces. No new bead schema fields are introduced.
50	No new top-level FEAT is created.
51	
52	### Policy
53	
54	Every automated bead attempt has two quality checkpoints:
55	
56	1. **Bead readiness assessment.** Before `ddx work` claims a bead or `ddx try`
57	   launches the implementation invocation, DDx evaluates whether the bead is
58	   tractable and actionable. It checks the bead description, acceptance
59	   criteria, labels, parent, dependency metadata, spec-id, prior attempt
60	   history, and the canonical rubric in
61	   `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`. The implementation hook
62	   may still advertise `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility only; the
63	   canonical product concept is bead readiness assessment.
64	2. **Post-attempt triage.** After an attempt finalizes, DDx triages the result
65	   against the same lifecycle quality policy so a low-quality prompt failure,
66	   missing rationale, empty review block, or structurally ambiguous outcome is
67	   classified and surfaced consistently.
68	
69	The readiness checkpoint and the post-attempt triage checkpoint are distinct:
70	readiness decides whether a bead should be claimed or rewritten before
71	execution, lint/rubric scoring measures prompt quality inside the readiness
72	evaluation, and triage classifies the attempt after evidence exists.
73	
74	Both checkpoints invoke the same nested bead-lifecycle workflow skill under the
75	`ddx` skill tree. The skill is responsible for translating the rubric into
76	agent-readable findings; DDx remains responsible for hook timing, evidence
77	placement, and outcome classification.
78	
79	Post-attempt triage classifications feed
80	[`TD-031`](../technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md). ADR-023 does not
81	define final queue mutation policy; TD-031 remains the source of truth for
82	whether an attempt closes, stays open for human triage, becomes blocked, is
83	superseded, or receives a retry cooldown.
84	
85	### Staged Rollout And Factory Mode
86	
87	The default rollout is WARN-ONLY.
88	
89	- WARN-ONLY mode reports the lint score, missing criteria, waiver matches, and
90	  suggested remediation, then proceeds with dispatch.
91	- BLOCK mode is opt-in until the queue baseline confirms that ordinary
92	  execution-ready beads can consistently satisfy the rubric.
93	- BLOCK mode may stop only on a valid low lint score after applicable rubric
94	  skips and label waivers are applied. It must not stop on hook crashes,
95	  missing skill files, transient filesystem errors, or malformed lint output.
96	- Reliable factory mode is BLOCK mode plus default adversarial review. In that
97	  mode, poorly specified work is improved, decomposed, or blocked before claim
98	  rather than allowed to consume implementation attempts.
99	
100	This makes the policy measurable before it becomes a dispatch gate and avoids
101	freezing the queue while legacy beads are being retrofitted.
102	
103	### Waiver Model
104	
105	Waivers exist at two levels:
106	
107	- **Rubric-level by bead type.** The skips documented in
108	  `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md` are built into lint:
109	  doc-only beads may omit criterion (c) and (d), epic beads may satisfy those
110	  criteria through children, and pure deletion or rename beads may omit
111	  criterion (d) while preserving behavior.
112	- **Per-bead labels.** Rare exceptions use labels of the form
113	  `lint-waiver:<criterion>`, for example `lint-waiver:c`. Labels are the
114	  durable waiver store because FEAT-004 already defines labels as free-form
115	  bead metadata and no schema change is warranted.
116	
117	Operator override uses `--force --reason <text>`. The override records an event
118	in the bead evidence stream naming the criterion, mode, actor, reason, and lint
119	summary. It does not silently bypass the hook and it does not mutate the rubric.
120	
121	### Fail-Open Behavior
122	
123	The lifecycle hooks follow reliability principle P1's fail-open posture for
124	infrastructure failures.
125	
126	- If the hook cannot run, the workflow skill is missing, output cannot be
127	  parsed, or evidence cannot be written, DDx records the infrastructure failure
128	  and proceeds.
129	- In WARN-ONLY mode, all valid lint results proceed after reporting.
130	- In BLOCK mode, only a valid post-waiver lint result below the configured
131	  threshold blocks dispatch.
132	- Post-attempt triage failures never erase the attempt result. They annotate the
133	  report and evidence so retry policy and operators can distinguish "agent made
134	  a bad attempt" from "quality infrastructure failed."
135	
136	### Safe Improvement, Decomposition, And Recovery UX
137	
138	Bead readiness assessment may update a bead before execution only when the
139	update is intent-preserving and grounded in durable context. Safe improvements
140	include normalizing the description into the authoring template, adding
141	discovered file:line evidence, adding an obvious subsystem test command, and
142	wiring deterministic labels, parent, or dependencies. Readiness must block for
143	human input instead of inventing acceptance criteria, changing scope, choosing
144	between conflicting requirements, or guessing a missing governing artifact.
145	
146	If readiness finds the bead too broad, it decomposes before claim. Every parent AC
147	must map to at least one child AC or be explicitly marked `needs_human` or
148	`non_scope`; token-overlap metrics are heuristics, not proof of preservation.
149	The parent is blocked/decomposed with child ids and the AC map in evidence.
150	
151	When BLOCK mode stops dispatch, the operator-facing output must be actionable.
152	It prints:
153	
154	- bead id, score, mode, and blocked criteria
155	- the missing fields or malformed sections in plain language
156	- the waiver labels already applied and the labels that would waive each
157	  failed criterion
158	- suggested `ddx bead update` commands for adding description, acceptance,
159	  labels, parent, notes, or dependency metadata
160	- the `--force --reason` form for exceptional dispatch, with the note that an
161	  evidence event will be recorded
162	
163	Recovery output must not require reading the lint implementation or an
164	out-of-band report to fix an ordinary authoring problem.
165	
166	## Consequences
167	
168	- FEAT-004 owns the label-based waiver storage and the no-new-schema rule.
169	- FEAT-010 owns hook insertion in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, attempt
170	  classification, and the `OutcomeReason` report field.
171	- FEAT-011 owns nested workflow-skill packaging so the same rubric guidance can
172	  be reused by lint, triage, review, breakdown, replay, and benchmark flows.
173	- Legacy beads can continue running in WARN-ONLY while the queue is baselined.
174	- Once BLOCK mode is enabled, dispatch quality becomes explicit and auditable
175	  instead of depending on operator memory.
176	
177	## Non-Goals
178	
179	- A new FEAT for bead quality; the existing feature specs own the affected
180	  surfaces.
181	- The separate `in_progress` eligibility bug.
182	- Cross-project skill packaging, which remains deferred to FEAT-015.
183	- New fields in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`; waivers use labels and evidence events.
184	
185	## References
186	
187	- `ddx-06b77652` — RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES bead for P7.
